items.title: Bangkok high-rise residents fear returning home after powerful quake | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>With the death toll in neighboring Myanmar still rising, rescue efforts to find survivors of a collapsed skyscraper in Bangkok were still underway on Saturday. 

Many residents, especially those living in high-rise condos, say they're afraid to go home. Local authorities have received at least 2,000 reports of damages in buildings and say they will run structural checks in the coming days.


#thailand #bangkok #earthquake 

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Dozens still trapped after 30-story Bangkok tower reduced to rubble by quake | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>The search for survivors continues after a 30-story building collapsed in Bangkok following a massive quake in Myanmar. There is a 72-hour window to reach those trapped, with some thought to be meters underground.

DW's Tommy Walker gave us the latest from the scene, and explained how hundreds of rescuers are still trying to search the rubble.


#thailand #bangkok #earthquake

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Myanmar asks for rare outside help after devastating earthquake | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Myanmar's military-led government says more than a thousand people have died after Friday's powerful earthquake. 
The 7.7 magnitude quake struck around midday - with its epicenter close to the city of Mandalay, followed by several aftershocks. Destructive tremors were also felt in neighboring Thailand. 
International support is now starting to arrive in Myanmar after a rare appeal for outside help from the country's military rulers. 

00:00 The latest from Myanmar and Thailand
03:02 DW speaks with journalist Teirra Kamolvattanavith in Bangkok
07:32 DW speaks with Will Rashou, Executive Director of the aid organization Save the Children in Thailand

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Why do we use the term 'The Global South'?
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>How to describe non-Western countries has evolved in recent years. Is one term fit the majority of the world's population and the 70-130 countries that are isometimes included in the term "The Global South"?  DW's security podcast, Global Eyes, takes a look. items.title: Istanbul rally draws thousands in support of Erdogan rival Imamoglu | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Protesters have gathered in Istanbul for a mass rally called by the country's main opposition after the arrest of the city's mayor, Ekrem Imamoglu.
The rally is the first such CHP-led gathering since Tuesday, and comes on the eve of the Muslim celebration of Eid - marking the end of Ramadan. Imamoglu's detention earlier this month sparked 10-days of protests - the biggest street demonstrations in more than a decade. He's widely seen as the only Turkish politician capable of challenging President Recep Tayyip Erdogan. DW's Julia Hahn reports from Istanbul.

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: How Trump’s economic team hopes to reset the international financial system | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Members of President Donald Trump's economic team are pushing for a total reorganization of the international financial system. The so-called "Mar-a-Lago accord" - named after the president's resort in Florida, aims to tilt the international economy in favor of the US. As part of the scheme, the White House would reclassify trading partners into friends and enemies, and deliberately devalue the US dollar.

00:00 How Trump advisers hope to coerce allies into a dollar devaluation and a US debt reset
03:11 Jessie Yin Assistant Director at the Atlantic Council's GeoEconomics Center talks DW through Trump’s competing goals

#trump #usdollar #usdebtcrisis 

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: How is Myanmar’s civil war affecting relief operations in the wake of the earthquake? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Rescuers are scrambling to find survivors trapped under the rubble of buildings and bridges, after Myanmar and Thailand were hit by a powerful earthquake. The epicenter of the magnitude 7.7 quake was near Myanmar's second biggest city, Mandalay which is home to more than 1.5 million people. An analysis by the US Geological Survey indicates that thousands of people could have been killed in Myanmar- but the official death toll in the country at this point is 144. The tremor also shook neighboring Thailand, where at least 9 were killed and dozens more are missing.

00:00 Rescuers searching for survivors after earthquake hits Myanmar and Thailand 
00:39 Scenes of destruction in the Thai capital Bangkok and Myanmar’s capital Naypyidaw
02:32 Theo Kidess, Head of Mission at Germany's Embassy in Yangon spoke to DW in a personal capacity. He told DW why the military junta has asked for international help so quickly this time.   

#myanmar #myanmarnews 

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Does the EU need its own defense structures? - Andrius Kubilius, Commissioner for Defense Interview
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Donald Trump's vow to make Greenland part of the United States is being eyed with disbelief in Europe - where it is seen as another crack in the transatlantic alliance. It also raises questions about Mr Trump's priorities for security and defense. DW spoke to the European Commissioner for Defense, Andrius Kubilius, and asked him whether Europe should now reconsider its commitment to NATO. 

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: US Vice President JD Vance accuses Denmark of under-investing in Greenland’s security | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>The US Vice President JD Vance has made a controversial one-day visit to the Danish territory  Greenland. Speaking at a US military base on the Arctic island, he accused Denmark of not doing enough to keep the people of Greenland safe from China and Russia.

00:00 US Vice President Vance accuses Denmark of under-investing in Greenland
02:39 Sophie Arts, a fellow with the German Marshal Fund in Washington DC, tells DW how Denmark protects Greenland.

#greenland #trump #vance

items.title: Turkey's government has staged a "coup" against the opposition, CHP leader Ozgur Ozel tells DW
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Protests continue in Turkey after Istanbul's Mayor Ekrem Imamoglu was jailed pending trial over allegations of graft. His arrest provoked the largest anti-government protests in more than a decade, and mass arrests nationwide. Imamoglu, from the main opposition Republican People's Party (CHP), leads President Recep Tayyip Erdogan in some polls. The CHP, other opposition parties, rights groups and Western powers all say the case against him is to eliminate a potential electoral threat to Erdogan. The government denies any influence over the judiciary and says the courts are independent. DW's Julia Hahn spoke to Ozgur Ozel, the leader of the main opposition.

#imamoğlu #turkey #erdoğan 
For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: 'Signs of supremacism are there' Former South African Ambassador to the US | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>DW spoke to former South African Ambassador to the US Ebrahim Rasool about his expulsion by the Trump administration. The US government accused him of being "a race-baiting politician." This followed earlier remarks the ambassador had made during a webinar hosted by a South African think tank. Cheering crowds welcomed Rasool in Cape Town when he returned home. He said, "we come home with no regrets." Rasool said that it was important for South Africa to fix its relationship with the US. President Donald Trump cut all funding to the African country alleging its government supports the Palestinian militant group Hamas and Iran and follows anti-white policies at home.  

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Can Europe afford to push back against Erdogan? | Berlin Briefing Podcast
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Turkey’s President Recep Tayyip Erdogan is becoming even more authoritarian. He has had his key challenger in the next elections, the mayor of Istanbul Ekrem Imamoglu arrested. Tens of thousands took to the streets. The European Union condemns the arrest but stops short of threatening any action. In this Berlin Briefing DW’s Chief Political Editor Michaela Küfner asks whether Europe can afford standing up to Erdogan? Her guests, the crisis reporter Can Merey and former EU ambassador to Turkey Marc Pierini map out why Germany and Europe are so highly dependent on Erdogan on defense and migration. Both warn that there are no easy options as Europe balances its democratic values against its immediate interests.

#BerlinBriefing #Turkey #Erdogan
For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Rescuers are searching for survivors trapped under the rubble of buildings and bridges after Myanmar and Thailand were hit by an earthquake. The epicenter of the magnitude 7.7 quake was near Myanmar's second-biggest city, Mandalay. An analysis by the US Geological Survey indicates that thousands of people could have been killed - but the official death toll currently stands at 144. The tremor also shook neighboring Thailand, where at least 6 people have been killed and dozens more are missing.
